advantages: Fixed seeds make A/B comparisons of models and hyperparameters trustworthy — you isolate intentional changes from shuffle noise.
Logged seeds plus pinned environments let teammates rerun experiments and validate reported metrics.
Deterministic splits and CV folds stabilize evaluation curves so you can detect real improvements over noise.
Environment pinning (lock files, Docker) reduces "works on my machine" drift between laptops, CI, and cloud GPUs.
Explicit reproducibility metadata strengthens audit trails for regulated or research-facing ML work.
disadvantages: Chasing bitwise-identical GPU outputs often requires slower deterministic kernels and limits performance.
Over-relying on a single seed can hide variance — some teams need multi-seed runs to estimate stability.
Pinning every dependency is maintenance-heavy; security patches and library updates break frozen stacks.
Seeds do not fix upstream data changes, API nondeterminism, or hardware differences (CPU vs GPU numerics).
Multiprocess data loading and third-party C extensions can introduce randomness seeds alone cannot control.
